title: Quasiperiodic Schroedinger operators with multiple frequencies
speaker: Wilhelm Schlag
abstract: Analysis and Beyond - Celebrating Jean Bourgain's Work and Impact

proof_stages.label: Ergodic Schrödinger setup and Lyapunov-direction heuristic
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up one-dimensional ergodic/quasiperiodic Schrödinger operators, deterministic spectral components, Anderson localization, transfer matrices, Lyapunov exponents, and the tempting but invalid Oseledets-direction proof of localization.

proof_stages.label: Bourgain-Goldstein localization theorem
proof_stages.summary: The Bourgain-Goldstein theorem is stated as almost-sure Anderson localization for analytic one-frequency quasiperiodic potentials with positive Lyapunov exponent, with comments on almost Mathieu and higher-torus extensions and on the need to control resonances.

proof_stages.label: Large-deviation control of finite-scale transfer matrices
proof_stages.summary: The proof obtains exponentially small exceptional sets for deviations of finite-scale log transfer-matrix norms from their mean by using subharmonic representation, Fourier decay, and almost invariance of products under phase shifts.

proof_stages.label: Green-function resonance reduction
proof_stages.summary: Finite-volume Green functions are declared good when the energy is separated from finite-volume spectrum and the inverse decays off-diagonal, and localization is reduced to showing that a resonant window near a generalized eigenfunction is not accompanied by separated bad windows.

proof_stages.label: Semi-algebraic elimination of double resonances
proof_stages.summary: Bad large-deviation sets are encoded as semi-algebraic sets of controlled degree, quantitative Tarski-Seidenberg projection bounds are invoked, and Bourgain-Goldstein's geometric lemma excludes frequent returns of the line x=l omega to the bad set for all but a small set of frequencies.

proof_stages.label: Avalanche principle and Lyapunov regularity
proof_stages.summary: The avalanche principle controls long products of matrices once pairwise cancellations are excluded, yielding quantitative convergence of finite-scale Lyapunov exponents, Hölder regularity of the integrated density of states, and higher-dimensional cocycle analogues under spectral separation hypotheses.

proof_stages.label: Cartan-Feshbach multiscale localization on higher-dimensional lattices
proof_stages.summary: For higher-dimensional lattice Schrödinger operators without transfer matrices, large coupling replaces positive Lyapunov exponent, while finite-volume boxes, Cartan estimates, matrix-valued Cartan bootstrapping, Feshbach reduction, and semi-algebraic number-theoretic counting are used to prevent long chains of resonant boxes.

proof_stages.label: Bourgain high-dimensional elimination and multi-frequency gap program
proof_stages.summary: Bourgain's later semi-algebraic elimination method confines higher-dimensional resonances without the previous number theory, and the speaker explains how it is being combined with one-frequency gap technology to prove finite-volume localization and eigenvalue separation toward the Chulaevsky-Sinai multi-frequency conjecture.

examples.name: Periodic crystals and random impurities
examples.note: The periodic-potential metal and Anderson random-impurity insulator are used as historical model cases motivating the spectral alternatives of absolutely continuous spectrum versus localization.
examples.engagement_reason: Periodic crystals and random impurities are cited as physical origins and known localization/ac-spectrum phenomena, with no concrete calculation on them.

passes.reason: The perfect periodic crystal has exact lattice regularity, while random impurities are used to show conducting/absolutely-continuous behavior can fail.

examples.name: Fourier proof for a smooth circle-rotation average
examples.note: A simple ergodic average of a C^2 function under a Diophantine rotation is used to reveal the strategy that must be adapted to rough subharmonic log-norm functions.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker outlines the Fourier-series proof for averaging a smooth circle-rotation orbit, including small denominators and Fourier-coefficient decay.

examples.note: The cosine-potential almost Mathieu operator supplies a visual instance of the subharmonic log-norm function whose downward spikes are controlled by the large-deviation estimate.
examples.engagement_reason: The almost Mathieu picture is interpreted in terms of negative log-norm spikes and how the large-deviation estimate measures their projected size.

examples.name: Diagonal multiplication operator at huge disorder
examples.note: Turning off the Laplacian leaves a purely diagonal operator, making the Green function decay condition immediate and thereby clarifying why it is a natural finite-volume nonresonance condition.
examples.engagement_reason: The huge-disorder diagonal multiplication model is concretely analyzed by dropping the Laplacian, inverting the diagonal operator, and noting zero off-diagonal Green-function decay.

passes.reason: Turning off the Laplacian gives the decoupled zero-hopping or infinite-disorder limiting case used as a simplifying model.

examples.name: Large matrices multiplying to the identity
examples.note: The toy product of large factors such as A and A^{-1} exposes the only obstruction the avalanche principle must rule out, namely pairwise cancellation of expanding and contracting directions.
examples.engagement_reason: The large-matrix product example is used concretely to show that huge factors such as a matrix and its inverse can multiply to the identity, motivating pairwise-cancellation control.

passes.reason: Large inverse factors with product equal to the identity realize maximal cancellation and obstruct naive norm-product estimates.
A product such as A A^{-1}=I is a maximally cancelling collapse to the symmetric identity, obstructing naïve norm control.
Large inverse factors whose product is the identity give maximal cancellation and refute the naive hope of estimating a product only from factor norms.
Products such as AA^{-1}=I give complete cancellation to a trivial product, obstructing naive product-norm control.
An inverse pair of large matrices collapses to the norm-minimal identity product, defeating any naive product-norm estimate.
Large factors arranged as A and A^{-1} collapse to the minimal product I, obstructing naive norm multiplication.
Huge neighboring factors whose product is the identity give the extreme cancellation case blocking naive norm multiplicativity.

Exact inverse cancellation of large factors to the identity is the trivial minimal-product obstruction to naive norm estimates.

examples.name: Concentrated versus evenly distributed Riesz mass
examples.note: The two extreme logarithmic-potential cases show why Cartan-type estimates can fail without distributional regularity but give large-deviation gains in favorable cases.
examples.engagement_reason: The concentrated and evenly distributed Riesz-mass cases are compared to determine whether Cartan-type estimates yield no large-deviation gain or a large gain.

passes.reason: A point mass and a uniform circular mass are collapsed and fully spread extreme distributions, with the point-mass case showing large-deviation estimates can fail.

A point-mass Riesz distribution and a uniform circular one are opposite extremes, with the point-mass case defeating automatic large-deviation gain.

examples.name: Two-point eigenvalue splitting and forbidden zones
examples.note: A tiny finite-dimensional Dirichlet problem with a double eigenvalue split by a perturbation is presented as the mechanism behind Sinai's forbidden-zone picture for opening spectral gaps.
examples.engagement_reason: The two-point model is worked through by computing the eigenvalue splitting when epsilon becomes positive and linking it to forbidden spectral zones.

examples.name: Shifted paraboloid intersection in two frequencies
examples.note: A surface such as a paraboloid shifted against itself explains geometrically why, in two-frequency situations, resonances generically occur along nonhorizontal curves and therefore need not open forbidden zones.
examples.engagement_reason: The shifted paraboloid example is geometrically reasoned through, with the intersection curve’s non-horizontality used to explain why forbidden zones do not open.
